summary: Muratori argues that while computing hardware has improved dramatically since 1990, software reliability has actually deteriorated. He identifies the lack of platform competition—duopolies on both desktop and phone—as a key driver, noting that OS vendors no longer treat operating systems as products but as vehicles for other business goals. The complexity of interfacing with consumer hardware creates an insurmountable barrier to new OS entrants, which also explains Linux's struggles on the desktop. He proposes that standardizing on stable instruction set architectures (ISAs) could be a first step toward fixing what he calls 'The Thirty Million Line Problem'—the overwhelming complexity that undermines software quality.
key_points: Software reliability has gotten worse since 1990 despite massive hardware improvements—mandatory updates, malware, ads, and instability are now the norm
Desktop and phone markets are each controlled by duopolies whose revenue doesn't depend on OS quality, so they optimize for other business goals instead of reliability
The complexity of hardware interfacing creates an insurmountable barrier to new operating systems, locking in the duopoly and explaining Linux's desktop struggles
Stable, simple ISAs could reduce the thirty-million-line driver/OS complexity and allow reliability-focused developers to build better software stacks
money_quotes: While features and variety have improved, trust and reliability has, if anything, hit an all-time low.
Unlike that of yesteryear, software working in the morning today may well suddenly stop working in the afternoon, even if the user has done absolutely nothing in the interim.

The operating system is never a product anymore — it is merely something users are forced to use based on the hardware they have chosen, and it is increasingly treated solely as a vehicle for pursuing the platform holders' other business goals.

The unchallenged duopoly on both major computing form factors is likely due to the fact that interfacing with consumer hardware is now so difficult that it would be impossible for any new operating system to enter the market.
While shipping a stable ISA is more expensive that shipping continually updated buggy drivers, the rewards both to hardware companies and to users could be significant.
key_insight: The complexity of modern hardware interfaces has created an unbreakable OS duopoly that prioritizes business goals over reliability, and only simplifying hardware abstraction through stable ISAs can break the cycle.
